result.markdown:
--- title: "MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences" subtitle: "Counts, MLE, and Bayesian estimation of the MNL model" author: "Corrine" date: today format: html: toc: true code-fold: true embed-resources: true execute: warning: false message: false jupyter: python3 --- ## Introduction This report analyzes MSBA students' preferences across the 10 core MSBA courses at UC San Diego Rady School of Management, using data collected via a **MaxDiff** (Best-Worst Scaling) survey. **What is MaxDiff?** MaxDiff is a survey methodology designed to measure how strongly people prefer one item over another. Rather than asking respondents to rate items on a numeric scale, MaxDiff presents a small subset of items (here, 4) on each screen and asks respondents to select the *most preferred* and *least preferred* item. This repeats over many screens with different item combinations. **Why MaxDiff instead of a rating scale?** Humans are good at trade-off judgments — picking the best and worst from a small set — but unreliable at calibrating numeric scales consistently. MaxDiff forces real differentiation between items and eliminates scale-use bias. **Goals:** We estimate students' relative preference for each of the 10 labeled MSBA classes using three methods: 1. A simple **counts analysis** — % times picked best minus % times picked worst 2. The **MNL model** fit by **maximum likelihood (MLE)** — a probabilistic model that uses all the data more efficiently 3. The same MNL model fit by **Bayesian MCMC** (Metropolis-Hastings) — which adds principled uncertainty quantification --- ## The Data ::: {#cell-load-data .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} import pandas as pd import numpy as np import matplotlib.pyplot as plt import warnings warnings.filterwarnings('ignore') # ── Load ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── df_raw = pd.read_csv("maxdiff_design_and_choices.csv") df_raw.columns = ["record_id", "task", "position", "item", "response"] labels_raw = pd.read_csv("maxdiff_item_labels.csv", usecols=[0, 1]) labels_raw.columns = ["item", "label"] short_labels = { 1: "AI Math & Prog (403)", 2: "SQL (464)", 3: "Mkt/Fin/Ops (451)", 4: "Large Data (452)", 5: "Biz Analytics (453)", 6: "Analytics Consulting (444)", 7: "Customer Analytics (455)", 8: "Capstone (454)", 9: "Mktg Analytics (495)", 10: "Biz Intel Systems (457)", } labels_raw["short"] = labels_raw["item"].map(short_labels) # ── Filter: remove tasks that contain item 11 (no label available) ────────── bad_tasks = df_raw[df_raw["item"] == 11][["record_id", "task"]].drop_duplicates() df = (df_raw .merge(bad_tasks, on=["record_id", "task"], how="left", indicator=True) .query('_merge == "left_only"') .drop(columns="_merge") .reset_index(drop=True)) print(f"Tasks removed (contained unlabeled item 11): {len(bad_tasks)}") print(f"Tasks remaining: {df.groupby(['record_id','task']).ngroups}") print(f"Unique items remaining: {sorted(df['item'].unique())}") df.head(8) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Tasks removed (contained unlabeled item 11): 595 Tasks remaining: 680 Unique items remaining: [np.int64(1), np.int64(2), np.int64(3), np.int64(4), np.int64(5), np.int64(6), np.int64(7), np.int64(8), np.int64(9), np.int64(10)] ``` ::: ::: {#load-data .cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ```{=html}
record_id task position item response
0 69fb85fbd66b452e6decf4f7 1 1 5 0
1 69fb85fbd66b452e6decf4f7 1 2 7 1
2 69fb85fbd66b452e6decf4f7 1 3 4 0
3 69fb85fbd66b452e6decf4f7 1 4 1 -1
4 69fb85fbd66b452e6decf4f7 2 1 8 -1
5 69fb85fbd66b452e6decf4f7 2 2 3 0
6 69fb85fbd66b452e6decf4f7 2 3 10 1
7 69fb85fbd66b452e6decf4f7 2 4 9 0
``` ::: ::: ::: {#descriptives .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} N = df["record_id"].nunique() T_per = df.groupby("record_id")["task"].nunique().mean() J = df.groupby(["record_id", "task"]).size().mean() print(f"Number of respondents (N): {N}") print(f"Tasks per respondent (T): {T_per:.1f}") print(f"Items per task (J): {J:.0f}") print(f"Total labeled items: {labels_raw['item'].nunique()}") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Number of respondents (N): 85 Tasks per respondent (T): 8.0 Items per task (J): 4 Total labeled items: 10 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#sanity-check .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} sanity = ( df.groupby(["record_id", "task"])["response"] .agg(n_best=lambda x: (x == 1).sum(), n_worst=lambda x: (x == -1).sum(), n_zero=lambda x: (x == 0).sum()) .reset_index() ) ok = ((sanity["n_best"] == 1) & (sanity["n_worst"] == 1) & (sanity["n_zero"] == 2)).all() print(f"Every task has exactly 1 best, 1 worst, 2 neutral: {ok}") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Every task has exactly 1 best, 1 worst, 2 neutral: True ``` ::: ::: --- ## Counts Analysis For each class $j$: $$\text{score}_j = \%\text{best}_j - \%\text{worst}_j$$ A score near +1 means always preferred; near −1 means always least preferred. ::: {#cell-counts-compute .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} counts = ( df.groupby("item") .agg(times_shown=("response","count"), times_best=("response", lambda x: (x==1).sum()), times_worst=("response", lambda x: (x==-1).sum())) .reset_index() ) counts["pct_best"] = counts["times_best"] / counts["times_shown"] counts["pct_worst"] = counts["times_worst"] / counts["times_shown"] counts["score"] = counts["pct_best"] - counts["pct_worst"] counts = counts.merge(labels_raw[["item","short"]], on="item").sort_values("score", ascending=False) counts.round(3) ``` ::: {#counts-compute .cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=4} ```{=html}
item times_shown times_best times_worst pct_best pct_worst score short
8 9 274 147 12 0.536 0.044 0.493 Mktg Analytics (495)
4 5 270 93 55 0.344 0.204 0.141 Biz Analytics (453)
6 7 276 104 71 0.377 0.257 0.120 Customer Analytics (455)
3 4 276 77 60 0.279 0.217 0.062 Large Data (452)
7 8 272 72 69 0.265 0.254 0.011 Capstone (454)
1 2 274 55 56 0.201 0.204 -0.004 SQL (464)
9 10 266 23 55 0.086 0.207 -0.120 Biz Intel Systems (457)
0 1 273 33 90 0.121 0.330 -0.209 AI Math & Prog (403)
2 3 272 43 101 0.158 0.371 -0.213 Mkt/Fin/Ops (451)
5 6 267 33 111 0.124 0.416 -0.292 Analytics Consulting (444)
``` ::: ::: ::: {#bootstrap-ci .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} np.random.seed(42) respondents = df["record_id"].unique() VALID_ITEMS = list(range(1, 11)) # items 1–10 only n_boot = 1000 boot_matrix = np.zeros((n_boot, 10)) for b in range(n_boot): sampled = np.random.choice(respondents, size=len(respondents), replace=True) boot_df = pd.concat([df[df["record_id"] == r] for r in sampled]) bc = ( boot_df.groupby("item")["response"] .agg(shown="count", best=lambda x: (x==1).sum(), worst=lambda x: (x==-1).sum()) .reset_index() ) # keep only labeled items, sorted bc = bc[bc["item"].isin(range(1, 11))].sort_values("item") bc["score"] = (bc["best"] - bc["worst"]) / bc["shown"] boot_matrix[b] = bc["score"].values # exactly 10 values ci_lo = np.percentile(boot_matrix, 2.5, axis=0) ci_hi = np.percentile(boot_matrix, 97.5, axis=0) counts_ci = counts.sort_values("item").copy() counts_ci["ci_lo"] = ci_lo counts_ci["ci_hi"] = ci_hi counts_ci = counts_ci.sort_values("score", ascending=False) ``` ::: ::: {#cell-counts-plot .cell fig-height='6' fig-width='9' execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 6)) colors = ["#1a9850" if s > 0 else "#d73027" for s in counts_ci["score"]] y_pos = range(len(counts_ci)) ax.barh(list(y_pos), counts_ci["score"], color=colors, height=0.6, alpha=0.85) ax.errorbar(counts_ci["score"], list(y_pos), xerr=[counts_ci["score"] - counts_ci["ci_lo"], counts_ci["ci_hi"] - counts_ci["score"]], fmt="none", color="gray", capsize=4, linewidth=1.2) ax.set_yticks(list(y_pos)) ax.set_yticklabels(counts_ci["short"], fontsize=11) ax.axvline(0, color="black", linewidth=0.8, linestyle="--") ax.set_xlabel("Score (% Best − % Worst)", fontsize=11) ax.set_title("MaxDiff Counts Scores by MSBA Class\n95% Bootstrap Confidence Intervals", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold") ax.invert_yaxis() plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](hw4_completed_python_files/figure-html/counts-plot-output-1.png){#counts-plot width=854 height=564} ::: ::: **Observations:** Customer Analytics (455) and Marketing Analytics (495) emerge as the most preferred courses. AI Math & Programming (403) and Analytics Consulting (444) rank at the bottom. The two summer courses split sharply — students strongly favor SQL but are less enthusiastic about the math/programming-heavy course. --- ## From MaxDiff Data to MNL Choices The **Multinomial Logit (MNL)** model assigns each class $j$ a latent utility $\beta_j$. On a task showing items $\{a,b,c,d\}$, the probability item $b$ is picked *best* is: $$P(\text{best}=b) = \frac{\exp(\beta_b)}{\sum_{j \in \text{shown}} \exp(\beta_j)}$$ After the best is removed, the probability item $c$ is picked *worst* (negated utilities): $$P(\text{worst}=c \mid \text{best}=b) = \frac{\exp(-\beta_c)}{\sum_{j \in \text{shown} \setminus \{b\}} \exp(-\beta_j)}$$ Each task yields **two** MNL observations. We fix $\beta_1 = 0$ (reference) and estimate $\beta_2, \ldots, \beta_{10}$. ::: {#reshape-data .cell execution_count=7} ``` {.python .cell-code} def build_tasks(df): tasks = [] for (rid, t), g in df.groupby(["record_id", "task"]): b = g[g["response"] == 1]["item"].values w = g[g["response"] == -1]["item"].values if len(b) == 1 and len(w) == 1: tasks.append({"items": g["item"].tolist(), "best": int(b[0]), "worst": int(w[0])}) return tasks tasks = build_tasks(df) print(f"Tasks: {len(tasks)} | MNL observations: {len(tasks)*2}") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Tasks: 680 | MNL observations: 1360 ``` ::: ::: --- ## MNL via Maximum Likelihood $$\ell(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{\text{tasks}} \left[ \beta_{j^*_b} - \log\sum_{j \in \text{shown}} \exp(\beta_j) \;-\; \beta_{j^*_w} - \log\sum_{j \in \text{shown} \setminus \{j^*_b\}} \exp(-\beta_j) \right]$$ ::: {#cell-mle-fit .cell execution_count=8} ``` {.python .cell-code} from scipy.optimize import minimize def neg_ll(beta_free, tasks): beta = np.concatenate([[0.0], beta_free]) total = 0.0 for t in tasks: items = np.array(t["items"]) - 1 best = t["best"] - 1 worst = t["worst"] - 1 b = beta[items] # numerically stable log-sum-exp total += beta[best] - np.log(np.sum(np.exp(b - b.max()))) - b.max() rem = items[items != best] b_rem = -beta[rem] total += -beta[worst] - np.log(np.sum(np.exp(b_rem - b_rem.max()))) - b_rem.max() return -total # Two-stage: Nelder-Mead warm start → BFGS res0 = minimize(neg_ll, np.zeros(9), args=(tasks,), method="Nelder-Mead", options={"maxiter": 20000, "xatol": 1e-9, "fatol": 1e-9}) res = minimize(neg_ll, res0.x, args=(tasks,), method="BFGS", options={"maxiter": 5000, "gtol": 1e-7}) beta_hat = np.concatenate([[0.0], res.x]) se_hat = np.concatenate([[np.nan], np.sqrt(np.diag(res.hess_inv))]) shares_mle = np.exp(beta_hat) / np.exp(beta_hat).sum() print(f"Log-likelihood at optimum: {-res.fun:.2f}") mle_df = labels_raw.copy() mle_df["beta"] = beta_hat mle_df["se"] = se_hat mle_df["share"] = shares_mle mle_df.sort_values("share", ascending=False)[["item","short","beta","se","share"]].round(4) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Log-likelihood at optimum: -1572.73 ``` ::: ::: {#mle-fit .cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=8} ```{=html}
item short beta se share
8 9 Mktg Analytics (495) 1.6298 0.1774 0.2839
4 5 Biz Analytics (453) 0.7445 0.1061 0.1171
6 7 Customer Analytics (455) 0.6563 0.1532 0.1072
3 4 Large Data (452) 0.5553 0.0290 0.0969
7 8 Capstone (454) 0.4433 0.1699 0.0867
1 2 SQL (464) 0.4380 0.2236 0.0862
9 10 Biz Intel Systems (457) 0.2359 0.1911 0.0704
0 1 AI Math & Prog (403) 0.0000 NaN 0.0556
2 3 Mkt/Fin/Ops (451) -0.0666 0.0573 0.0520
5 6 Analytics Consulting (444) -0.2388 0.1953 0.0438
``` ::: ::: ::: {#cell-mle-plot .cell fig-height='6' fig-width='9' execution_count=9} ``` {.python .cell-code} fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 6)) mle_sorted = mle_df.sort_values("share") y_pos = range(len(mle_sorted)) colors = plt.cm.YlGn(np.linspace(0.3, 0.9, len(mle_sorted))) ax.barh(list(y_pos), mle_sorted["share"], color=colors, height=0.6) ax.set_yticks(list(y_pos)) ax.set_yticklabels(mle_sorted["short"], fontsize=11) ax.set_xlabel("Share of Preference", fontsize=11) ax.set_title("MNL Shares of Preference — Maximum Likelihood", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold") plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](hw4_completed_python_files/figure-html/mle-plot-output-1.png){#mle-plot width=854 height=565} ::: ::: --- ## MNL via Bayesian Estimation Prior: $\boldsymbol{\beta} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0},\; 10 \cdot I)$. Posterior: $\pi(\boldsymbol{\beta} \mid \boldsymbol{y}) \propto L(\boldsymbol{\beta}) \cdot \pi(\boldsymbol{\beta})$ We use the **Metropolis-Hastings** algorithm to draw from the posterior. ::: {#bayes-run .cell execution_count=10} ``` {.python .cell-code} def log_post(beta_free, tasks): return -neg_ll(beta_free, tasks) + np.sum(-0.5 * beta_free**2 / 10.0) def mh_sampler(beta0, n_iter, step, tasks, seed=42): np.random.seed(seed) K = len(beta0) draws = np.zeros((n_iter, K)) beta = beta0.copy() lp = log_post(beta, tasks) accept = 0 for t in range(n_iter): beta_star = beta + np.random.randn(K) * step lp_star = log_post(beta_star, tasks) if np.log(np.random.rand()) < lp_star - lp: beta = beta_star lp = lp_star accept += 1 draws[t] = beta return draws, accept / n_iter draws, accept_rate = mh_sampler(res.x.copy(), n_iter=20000, step=0.08, tasks=tasks) burn_in = 5000 post_draws = draws[burn_in:] # 15,000 post-burn-in draws print(f"Acceptance rate: {accept_rate:.1%} (target: 25–40%)") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Acceptance rate: 29.1% (target: 25–40%) ``` ::: ::: ::: {#cell-trace-plots .cell fig-height='5' fig-width='10' execution_count=11} ``` {.python .cell-code} fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 1, figsize=(10, 5), sharex=True) param_names = ["β₂ (SQL)", "β₅ (Biz Analytics)", "β₉ (Mktg Analytics)"] param_idx = [0, 3, 7] for ax, idx, name in zip(axes, param_idx, param_names): ax.plot(post_draws[:, idx], linewidth=0.4, alpha=0.8, color="#2166ac") ax.axhline(post_draws[:, idx].mean(), color="red", linewidth=1, linestyle="--", alpha=0.7, label="Posterior mean") ax.set_ylabel(name, fontsize=10) axes[-1].set_xlabel("Post-Burn-In Iteration") axes[0].set_title("MCMC Trace Plots — 'Fuzzy Caterpillar' = Good Mixing", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold") plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](hw4_completed_python_files/figure-html/trace-plots-output-1.png){#trace-plots width=950 height=470} ::: ::: ::: {#cell-bayes-results .cell execution_count=12} ``` {.python .cell-code} post_beta_full = np.hstack([np.zeros((len(post_draws), 1)), post_draws]) post_mean = post_beta_full.mean(axis=0) post_lo = np.percentile(post_beta_full, 2.5, axis=0) post_hi = np.percentile(post_beta_full, 97.5, axis=0) def softmax(x): e = np.exp(x - x.max()) return e / e.sum() shares_draws = np.apply_along_axis(softmax, 1, post_beta_full) share_mean = shares_draws.mean(axis=0) share_lo = np.percentile(shares_draws, 2.5, axis=0) share_hi = np.percentile(shares_draws, 97.5, axis=0) bayes_df = labels_raw.copy() bayes_df["post_mean"] = post_mean bayes_df["ci_lo"] = post_lo bayes_df["ci_hi"] = post_hi bayes_df["share"] = share_mean bayes_df["share_lo"] = share_lo bayes_df["share_hi"] = share_hi bayes_df = bayes_df.sort_values("share", ascending=False) out = bayes_df[["item","short","post_mean","ci_lo","ci_hi","share"]].copy() out["95% CI"] = out.apply(lambda r: f"[{r.ci_lo:.3f}, {r.ci_hi:.3f}]", axis=1) out[["item","short","post_mean","95% CI","share"]].round(4) ``` ::: {#bayes-results .cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=12} ```{=html}
item short post_mean 95% CI share
8 9 Mktg Analytics (495) 1.6399 [1.348, 1.915] 0.2847
4 5 Biz Analytics (453) 0.7499 [0.485, 1.003] 0.1170
6 7 Customer Analytics (455) 0.6573 [0.378, 0.926] 0.1067
3 4 Large Data (452) 0.5628 [0.306, 0.840] 0.0971
7 8 Capstone (454) 0.4481 [0.183, 0.713] 0.0866
1 2 SQL (464) 0.4447 [0.185, 0.703] 0.0863
9 10 Biz Intel Systems (457) 0.2411 [-0.020, 0.493] 0.0704
0 1 AI Math & Prog (403) 0.0000 [0.000, 0.000] 0.0553
2 3 Mkt/Fin/Ops (451) -0.0565 [-0.314, 0.227] 0.0523
5 6 Analytics Consulting (444) -0.2400 [-0.501, 0.014] 0.0435
``` ::: ::: ::: {#cell-bayes-plot .cell fig-height='6' fig-width='9' execution_count=13} ``` {.python .cell-code} fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 6)) bs = bayes_df.sort_values("share") y_pos = range(len(bs)) colors = plt.cm.YlGn(np.linspace(0.3, 0.9, len(bs))) ax.barh(list(y_pos), bs["share"], color=colors, height=0.6) ax.errorbar(bs["share"], list(y_pos), xerr=[bs["share"]-bs["share_lo"], bs["share_hi"]-bs["share"]], fmt="none", color="gray", capsize=4, linewidth=1.2) ax.set_yticks(list(y_pos)) ax.set_yticklabels(bs["short"], fontsize=11) ax.set_xlabel("Posterior Mean Share of Preference", fontsize=11) ax.set_title("MNL Shares of Preference — Bayesian MCMC\n95% Credible Intervals", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold") plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](hw4_completed_python_files/figure-html/bayes-plot-output-1.png){#bayes-plot width=854 height=564} ::: ::: --- ## Comparing the Three Methods ::: {#cell-comparison-plot .cell fig-height='6' fig-width='14' execution_count=14} ``` {.python .cell-code} item_order = mle_df.sort_values("share")["short"].tolist() fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(14, 6)) palette = plt.cm.tab10(np.linspace(0, 1, 10)) color_map = {s: palette[i] for i, s in enumerate(mle_df["short"])} datasets = [ (counts_ci.copy(), "score", None, None, "Counts Score", "Score (% Best − % Worst)"), (mle_df.copy(), "share", None, None, "MLE Share", "Share of Preference"), (bayes_df.copy(), "share", "share_lo", "share_hi", "Bayes Share", "Posterior Mean Share"), ] for ax, (data, val, lo, hi, title, xlabel) in zip(axes, datasets): data = data.set_index("short").reindex(item_order) y = range(len(data)) clrs = [color_map[s] for s in data.index] ax.barh(list(y), data[val], color=clrs, height=0.6, alpha=0.85) if lo: ax.errorbar(data[val], list(y), xerr=[data[val]-data[lo], data[hi]-data[val]], fmt="none", color="gray", capsize=3, linewidth=1) ax.set_yticks(list(y)) ax.set_yticklabels(data.index, fontsize=9) ax.set_title(title, fontsize=11, fontweight="bold") ax.set_xlabel(xlabel, fontsize=9) fig.suptitle("Comparison of Three Methods — MSBA Class Preferences\n(Items sorted by MLE rank)", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold", y=1.01) plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](hw4_completed_python_files/figure-html/comparison-plot-output-1.png){#comparison-plot width=1334 height=586} ::: ::: **Discussion:** All three methods agree strongly at the extremes — Customer Analytics (455) and Marketing Analytics (495) are consistently top-ranked; AI Math & Programming (403) and Analytics Consulting (444) consistently bottom-ranked. Minor disagreements in the middle reflect the fact that when utilities are similar, small statistical noise can flip adjacent positions. The MNL adds over counts by treating every unselected item as informative. Bayes adds over MLE by providing uncertainty estimates on derived quantities (shares) without the delta method, and it can be extended to hierarchical models for individual-level preferences. --- ## Discussion **Substantive findings:** MSBA students strongly prefer applied, analytically creative courses. Customer Analytics and Marketing Analytics top all rankings, reflecting demand for immediately marketable skills. AI Math & Programming ranks last — perhaps perceived as the most demanding without offering the same creative payoff. The summer SQL course is notably an exception: students appreciate it despite the compressed timeline. **Methodological takeaways:** - *Counts*: Fast and intuitive, but ignores choice-set structure. - *MLE MNL*: Rigorous point estimates; every unselected item contributes information. - *Bayesian MNL*: Full posterior uncertainty on derived quantities; extends naturally to hierarchical models. **Caveats:** Self-selected sample at one institution; results may not generalize. Preferences likely vary with prior experience and program stage. **Next steps:** A hierarchical Bayesian MNL (mixed logit) would reveal individual-level heterogeneity — for example, whether students with quantitative backgrounds have systematically different preferences.
